I have been through one category, four storm ( Hugo)South Carolina, and two category three storms.( Fran & Floyd)North Carolina. Since moving to Florida, I. endured Irma, Ian,iIdalia, Debbie, Helene, and now Milton. There is a great difference in every hurricane, even if they are in the same category. For example, hurricane Fran was more a wind event with a lot of tree and building damage. Hurricane Floyd was the same category as Fran, but it was more a flooding event. Major flooding in rural areas drowned a lot of livestock and farm animals and caused a lot of damage .It all depends on whether you’re getting a direct hit and the intensity of the storm that’s hitting you. If you’re near the water then that’s a whole other ballgame.. depending on how the storm moves in and the storm surge are all factors that could be life-threatening. In the category three hurricanes, it is mostly pine trees that go down first. One can actually hear the trees popping and cracking. In Strong category three and category four hurricanes, very large oak trees can be uprooted. In a category, three shingles, typically around the edges of the roof get ripped off down to the plywood. Trees can be blown down, cutting or slicing through homes. in a strong category, three sheds can be blown apart and blown across properties.. insulation from roof damage blown into trees and telephone poles coming down. Transformers exploding on the poles. Significant power outages. One might have to use pool water flush ones toilet. A category four hurricane you see many ,many trees down damage to homes, windows blown out on older homes. One must consider that hurricanes have a tendency to produce funnel clouds, which may or may not touch the ground and do additional damage. I am not even going to comment on flooding issues. If one lives near a river or a coastline, it is imperative that you know your evacuation zone And paste strict attention to storm surge warnings in your area
